title: Westpac director Nash latest KPMG casualty
full_text:
The storm surrounding KPMG has claimed another scalp, with Westpac director Peter Nash leaving the bank’s board immediately in a bid to “limit any ongoing distraction” from his link to the scandal surrounding the beleaguered audit and consulting firm.
The departure comes as KPMG confirmed that former SBS chief executive Michael Ebeid would become the firm’s inaugural independent chair, replacing Martin Sheppard.
Westpac announced on Wednesday said Mr Nash would leave, almost a month after The Australian revealed he had slept over at the house of Mr Sheppard as the firm pitched for the lucrative contract to conduct the audit of the banking major. The Australian does not suggest any wrongdoing.
Westpac chairman Steven Gregg said Mr Nash had been a keen advocate for the bank, “dealing with the many challenges the organisation faced during this time”. “I thank him for his significant contribution,” Mr Gregg said. But he noted Mr Nash’s continued attention on his time at KPMG and connections to the firm. “With recent attention on Peter’s former roles and relationships at KPMG, he has decided now is the right time to retire from the board to limit any ongoing distraction for the company,” Mr Gregg said.
Mr Nash worked at KPMG for 25 years and chaired the firm from 2011 to 2017. KPMG is facing a public reckoning over allegations its senior audit staff misused confidential client documents from the likes of Lendlease and Dexus to pitch for work at rivals. The Australian Securities & Investments Commission is investigating the scandal, with two formal probes into KPMG staff as well as several other investigations afoot.
Mr Nash stayed at Mr Sheppard’s home during the time KPMG was pitching for and subsequently won the audit contract for Westpac. Mr Nash told The Australian “I won’t be talking about that at all. This is a very sensitive matter” when contacted by this publication in March over the incident.
While not involved in the Westpac subcommittee that considered the bank’s audit contracts, Mr Nash also sat in on meetings with KPMG, EY and Deloitte as they pitched the bank.
Mr Gregg said Westpac held a “robust” audit tender process. “Peter declared his past connections with KPMG and was not on the selection committee, however he acknowledges the perception of bias that may have been created by his relationships,” he said. Mr Nash said Westpac had “changed significantly” over his eight years on the bank’s board. He will be replaced as chair of Westpac board audit committee by former Lendlease chair Michael Ullmer.
